questions.title: The Offside Trigger
questions.scenario: The referee calls offside on what you're SURE was a perfectly timed run. Your kid had a clear breakaway. You…

options.text: Groan internally and mutter “come on…” under your breath.
Stand up and yell “THAT WAS ONSIDE!” loud enough for three fields to hear.
Cup your hands and shout “Did you forget your glasses, ref?!”
Launch a detailed play-by-play roasting the ref's every call like you're hosting a podcast from the sideline.

questions.reveal: RAP Level 1 — Verbal Taunting “Did you forget your glasses?” is a textbook example of Level 1 abuse under U.S. Soccer’s RAP policy — minimum 2-game suspension. Also, the ref you’re yelling at has a 72% chance of being under 19 years old. So… you may be roasting a high schooler.

questions.title: The Post-Game Parking Lot
questions.scenario: Your kid's team just lost 2-1 on a controversial penalty call. The final whistle blew 5 minutes ago. You…

options.text: Tell your kid “great effort” and head to get ice cream. Life goes on.
Linger near the field packing up chairs extra slowly, making sure the ref feels the weight of your disapproving head-shake.
Wait by your car for a “calm, adult conversation” with the referee about that penalty call.
Follow the referee to their car to deliver your full halftime analysis in person.

questions.reveal: RAP Physical Level 2 — Confrontation “Following the referee to their car” is explicitly listed as Physical Level 2 abuse — minimum 10-game suspension. In one documented case, parents confronted a ref so aggressively in a parking lot that he reached for softball bats in self-defense. Over ice cream, nobody reaches for weapons.

questions.title: The Social Media Referee Critic
questions.scenario: Someone posts a video clip of a controversial call from your kid's game on the league's Facebook group. You…

options.text: Keep scrolling. You have a life outside youth soccer. (Allegedly.)
Comment “terrible call 🤦” and move on with your day.
Share the video with a 4-paragraph essay tagging the league, demanding accountability.
Track down the referee’s personal social media and post the clip on their page with a detailed performance review.

questions.reveal: RAP Level 3 — Threats & Cyberbullying Posting attacks on a referee’s personal social media is classified as Level 3 abuse — the same tier as parking-lot threats. Minimum 6-game and/or 6–24 month suspension. After Euro 2004, referee Urs Meier received 16,000 threatening emails and needed police protection. Let that sink in.

questions.title: The Selective Hearing Test
questions.scenario: It's the third foul called on your team this half, but you haven't seen a single one called the other way. You…

options.text: Consider that maybe, just maybe, your perspective from behind the cooler isn’t as accurate as the referee’s on-field view.
Say to the parent next to you: “This ref is calling it a bit one-sided, huh?”
Yell “CALL IT BOTH WAYS, REF!” loud enough to briefly pause play on the adjacent field.
Shout “What’s the other team paying you?!” and wait for laughs that never come.

questions.reveal: RAP Level 1 — Verbal Taunting “What’s the other team paying you?” is cited in the RAP program as a direct example of abuse. Research also shows that parents’ lack of knowledge of the Laws of the Game is a top driver of confrontation. Most “missed calls” aren’t missed at all — you just don’t know the rule.

questions.title: The Emotional Thermometer
questions.scenario: It's a tied game, 5 minutes left. A call goes against your team. Rate your most likely PEAK reaction:

options.text: One sarcastic clap. Then you sit back down like a functioning adult.
Stand up, throw your arms out, and yell “OH COME ON!” while spilling your coffee.
Pace the sideline yelling things your mother would not approve of.
March toward the referee screaming obscenities, veins fully visible. Your Fitbit thinks you’re doing HIIT.

questions.reveal: RAP Level 2 — Harassment / Intimidation Profanity-laden outbursts directed at a referee are Level 2 abuse — minimum 4-game suspension. The RAP program warns that unchecked verbal abuse “takes an emotional toll and has the potential to escalate into physically dangerous situations.” Your Fitbit was right to be concerned.

questions.title: The Youth Ref Reality Check
questions.scenario: A referee makes a bad call at your 10-year-old's game. Before you react, do you consider how old the referee might be?

options.text: Yes — that ref looks about 14, so I keep my mouth completely shut and let them learn.
I notice they’re young, so I downgrade from yelling to aggressive sighing.
Age doesn’t matter — a bad call is a bad call, and someone needs to tell them.
Wait, these referees are kids?! I thought they were just short adults who like whistles.

questions.reveal: Youth Referee Data 72% of U.S. youth soccer referees are under 19. In Minnesota, 69% are under 14. In Tennessee, 60% of 3,500 referees are minors. Under the RAP policy, abuse targeting minor referees carries TRIPLE the standard penalty. You may literally be screaming at a 13-year-old who’s doing this for pizza money.

questions.title: The Halftime Expert
questions.scenario: You're coaching and the ref has missed several calls in the first half. Halftime arrives. You…

options.text: Remind your players to focus on what they can control — which is not the referee.
Calmly approach the ref: “Hey, can you watch for contact in the box second half?”
Stand directly in the ref’s walking path and say “We need to talk” with the energy of a disappointed principal.
Get in the ref’s personal space and loudly list every first-half mistake so both teams’ parents can enjoy the show.

questions.reveal: RAP Level 2 — Intimidation Using body language to make a referee feel physically unsafe — blocking their path, invading their space — is Level 2 Harassment/Intimidation. Minimum 4-game suspension for coaches. Research also shows that when coaches model disrespect, spectators and players follow. Your sideline energy is contagious — in the worst way.

questions.title: The Red Card Meltdown
questions.scenario: Your star player gets a red card you believe is completely unjustified. In the heat of the moment, which of these has ever crossed your mind — even if you didn't say it out loud?

options.text: “I disagree, but the ref’s decision is final. Let’s regroup.”
“This ref is having an absolutely terrible game.”
“You’re gonna REGRET that call, ref!”
“I’ll see you in the parking lot.” (Said coolly, like a movie villain.)

questions.reveal: RAP Level 3 — Threats Both “You’re gonna regret that” and “I’ll see you in the parking lot” are specifically cited as Level 3 abuse — minimum 6-game and/or 6–24 month suspension. Threats can also lead to criminal charges. More than 20 U.S. states now have specific laws criminalizing sports official abuse. Your parking-lot tough talk could come with a court date.

questions.title: The Sideline Identity
questions.scenario: Be brutally honest. Which sideline archetype describes you best?

options.text: “The Silent Supporter” — you clap, cheer your kid’s name, and bring top-tier snacks. Refs don’t know you exist.
“The Anxious Commentator” — you narrate the entire game under your breath, occasionally spilling into audible territory.
“The Assistant Referee” — you call offside from 40 yards away, signal throw-ins, and helpfully correct the ref on every decision.
“The Main Character” — by halftime, more people are watching you than the actual game.

questions.reveal: Referee Retention Data 80% of new referees quit within 2 years, and unruly spectators are the #1 reason cited. The irony is staggering: the people who make the game possible (refs) are driven out by the people who can’t play the game without them. If you’re “The Main Character,” the sequel is called “Game Canceled Due to No Refs.”

questions.title: The Morning After
questions.scenario: After a game where you were… let’s say ‘vocal’ toward the referee, the next morning you…

options.text: Feel fine. You clapped, you cheered, you kept it clean. No regrets.
Feel a twinge of “maybe I was a bit much” and quietly resolve to dial it back.
Retell the story at work like a war hero: “You should’ve HEARD this ref…” — expecting validation.
Already composing a formal league complaint email at 7 AM. Subject line: “Urgent — Referee Incompetence.”

questions.reveal: Self-Reflection Check 90% of referees say abuse has gotten worse in the past 5 years. If your Monday morning starts with a referee complaint email, consider this: would you behave the same way toward your kid’s teacher? Their pediatrician? The person refereeing your kid’s game deserves the same respect — and statistically, they might BE a kid themselves.

tiers.title: 🟢 The Referee's Dream
tiers.desc: You’re the rarest species in youth soccer: a supportive, respectful sideline presence. Referees don’t know your name — and that’s the highest compliment. Keep being the example everyone else needs.

tiers.title: 🟡 Mostly Harmless
tiers.desc: You have your moments — an eye-roll here, a dramatic sigh there — but you catch yourself before crossing the line. The ref might notice you, but they’re not worried about you. Room for improvement, but you’re not the problem.

tiers.title: 🟡 Yellow Card Territory
tiers.desc: You’re edging into dissent. Your sideline behavior is noticeable and occasionally disruptive. The ref is aware of your presence — and not in a good way. Under the RAP policy, some of your habits would trigger formal warnings. Time to recalibrate.

tiers.title: 🟠 Red Card Energy
tiers.desc: You’re in official abuse territory under U.S. Soccer’s Referee Abuse Prevention policy. Your behavior would earn game suspensions. The ref remembers your face — and tells other refs about you. You’re part of the reason 80% of new refs quit.

tiers.title: 🔴 Parking Lot Problems
tiers.desc: You ARE the referee crisis. Your behavior falls into Level 2–3 of the RAP framework — the kind that causes refs to walk away from the sport permanently. Games get canceled because of people with your score. This is your wake-up call.

tiers.title: ⛔ Lifetime Ban Loading…
tiers.desc: Under the RAP policy, you’d face 6–24 month suspensions or a lifetime ban. You represent the extreme end of the abuse spectrum. You are actively making youth soccer worse for every child, referee, and family. Seriously — get help, attend a referee game, and start over.
